state_description: Standard diesel-electric locomotive fleet (AC traction; GE ES44/Evolution, Wabtec FLXdrive, EMD SD70) covering Australian heavy-haul and intermodal freight rail. Predominantly diesel with small electric component on electrified corridors (5% by energy). National average calibrated to AES 2023-24 estimated rail freight (25 PJ / 700 billion_tkm = 35,715 GJ/billion_tkm). Dominant operators: Aurizon, Pacific National, BHP (iron ore), Rio Tinto, FMG (Pilbara); Hunter Valley coal networks.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per billion tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: AES 2023-24: 25 PJ / 700 billion_tkm = 35,715 GJ/billion_tkm. Diesel 95% (33929 GJ), electricity 5% (1786 GJ). 2050: diesel 28,500, electricity 1,500 GJ/billion_tkm (efficiency improvement).
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 diesel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Green hydrogen combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for rail freight.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting locomotive fleet replacement cycles (~30-40 yr), high capital cost of rail electrification, and dominant role of private heavy-haul operators (BHP, Rio Tinto, Aurizon, Pacific National).
evidence_summary: AES 2023-24 estimated rail freight share; BITRE freight linehaul; NGGI rail freight.
derivation_method: Energy from AES 2023-24 estimated rail freight (25.0 PJ) / BITRE 700 billion_tkm = 35715 GJ/billion_tkm. 95% diesel (33929 GJ), 5% electricity (1786 GJ). Scope 1 CO2e: 2321 tCO2e/billion_tkm × 700 / 1e6 = 1.62 MtCO2e.
review_notes: AES does not separately report freight vs passenger rail energy. The 25 PJ estimate is derived by subtracting estimated passenger energy (8 PJ) from total rail AES figure. Phase 2: obtain direct NGERS freight rail reporting.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Disaggregate into heavy-haul (Pilbara, Hunter Valley), intermodal, and grain rail sub-families.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to freight rail demand technology.

state_description: Overhead electrification of high-density freight corridors. Electric traction is approximately 30% more efficient than diesel per tonne-km. Energy intensity 25,000 GJ electricity/billion_tkm (2025) improving to 20,000 GJ/billion_tkm (2050) via regenerative braking on grades. Limited to high-traffic corridors where capital cost (AUD 1-3M/km OHL) is justified. Zero scope 1 CO2e.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per billion tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: Electric freight loco: 25,000 GJ electricity/billion_tkm = 0.025 MJ/tkm. ~30% more efficient than diesel. Improving to 20,000 GJ/billion_tkm (2050). Scope 1 CO2e = 0; electricity scope 2 excluded per A003.
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 diesel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Green hydrogen combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for rail freight.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting locomotive fleet replacement cycles (~30-40 yr), high capital cost of rail electrification, and dominant role of private heavy-haul operators (BHP, Rio Tinto, Aurizon, Pacific National).
evidence_summary: Pilbara private rail (FMG, BHP, Rio) electrification studies; Network Rail UK electric freight data; ARENA heavy rail electrification analysis.
derivation_method: Energy from AES 2023-24 estimated rail freight (25.0 PJ) / BITRE 700 billion_tkm = 35715 GJ/billion_tkm. 95% diesel (33929 GJ), 5% electricity (1786 GJ). Scope 1 CO2e: 2321 tCO2e/billion_tkm × 700 / 1e6 = 1.62 MtCO2e.
review_notes: OHL electrification has very high capital costs. Max share of 30% by 2050 is limited to the Hunter Valley and Pilbara corridors. Most intermodal routes are unlikely to be electrified in Phase 1 timeframe.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add explicit corridor-by-corridor feasibility and capital cost model.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to overhead electrification rail freight demand node.

state_label: Hydrogen fuel cell locomotive — rail freight
state_description: Hydrogen fuel cell and battery hybrid locomotive for non-electrified freight routes. H2 energy intensity higher than diesel due to conversion losses and weight penalty of H2 storage (40,000 GJ H2/billion_tkm in 2030 improving to 30,000 GJ H2/billion_tkm by 2050 with technology maturity). Zero scope 1 CO2e with green hydrogen. Available from 2030.
cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per billion tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: H2 FC locomotive: 0 GJ/billion_tkm before 2030 (not available commercially). 2030: 40,000 GJ H2/billion_tkm (heavier loco, H2 storage penalty). Improving to 30,000 GJ H2/billion_tkm (2050). Scope 1 CO2e = 0.
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 diesel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Green hydrogen combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for rail freight.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting locomotive fleet replacement cycles (~30-40 yr), high capital cost of rail electrification, and dominant role of private heavy-haul operators (BHP, Rio Tinto, Aurizon, Pacific National).
availability_conditions: Available from 2030; zero coefficients and max_share in 2025.
evidence_summary: Wabtec FLXdrive hydrogen locomotive programme; CRRC H2 freight locomotive; ARENA hydrogen rail assessment; CSIRO National Hydrogen Roadmap.
derivation_method: Energy from AES 2023-24 estimated rail freight (25.0 PJ) / BITRE 700 billion_tkm = 35715 GJ/billion_tkm. 95% diesel (33929 GJ), 5% electricity (1786 GJ). Scope 1 CO2e: 2321 tCO2e/billion_tkm × 700 / 1e6 = 1.62 MtCO2e.
review_notes: H2 freight locomotives are pre-commercial as of 2025. The Wabtec FLXdrive and equivalent programmes are at demonstration phase. Green H2 supply at mine-site or rail corridor scale is a binding constraint.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add H2 refuelling station infrastructure; distinguish on-board compressed vs liquid H2.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to hydrogen freight locomotive demand node.

cost_components_summary: Annualised non-fuel operating cost per billion tonne-kilometres (real 2024 AUD), excluding explicit energy commodity purchases and carbon costs.
input_basis_notes: H2 FC locomotive: 0 GJ/billion_tkm before 2030 (not available commercially). 2030: 40,000 GJ H2/billion_tkm (heavier loco, H2 storage penalty). Improving to 30,000 GJ H2/billion_tkm (2050). Scope 1 CO2e = 0.
emissions_boundary_notes: Direct scope 1 diesel combustion only. Electricity upstream (scope 2) excluded; captured by the electricity supply family. Green hydrogen combustion CO2 = 0. No process emissions for rail freight.
rollout_limit_notes: National adoption bound reflecting locomotive fleet replacement cycles (~30-40 yr), high capital cost of rail electrification, and dominant role of private heavy-haul operators (BHP, Rio Tinto, Aurizon, Pacific National).
availability_conditions: Available from 2030; zero coefficients and max_share in 2025.
evidence_summary: Wabtec FLXdrive hydrogen locomotive programme; CRRC H2 freight locomotive; ARENA hydrogen rail assessment; CSIRO National Hydrogen Roadmap.
derivation_method: Energy from AES 2023-24 estimated rail freight (25.0 PJ) / BITRE 700 billion_tkm = 35715 GJ/billion_tkm. 95% diesel (33929 GJ), 5% electricity (1786 GJ). Scope 1 CO2e: 2321 tCO2e/billion_tkm × 700 / 1e6 = 1.62 MtCO2e.
review_notes: H2 freight locomotives are pre-commercial as of 2025. The Wabtec FLXdrive and equivalent programmes are at demonstration phase. Green H2 supply at mine-site or rail corridor scale is a binding constraint.
candidate_expansion_pathway: Add H2 refuelling station infrastructure; distinguish on-board compressed vs liquid H2.
times_or_vedalang_mapping_notes: Maps to hydrogen freight locomotive demand node.
